report.verdict: From the compliance-litigator lens: the subject's five controls read as internally well-documented (the skill files repeatedly pre-admit their own structural limits, e.g. 'STRUCTURAL ONLY,' 'human-enforced, not schema-checkable'), but the specific enforcement mechanism that C1/C5 lean on to defend against a fluent-narrative bypass -- human PR review -- has zero live teeth on the exact artifact under evaluation: PR #35 has no branch protection, no required reviewers, zero reviews, and a failing DCO check that does not block merge. The mechanical validator for C4 (failure_chain) is real and well-built as a schema, but it is proven only against committed synthetic fixtures -- it has never once run against a genuine production ledger entry, so the dossier's 'PASS' line is narrower evidence than it reads. A minor but concrete integrity gap: the dossier's own VERIFIED-premises section misdescribes the DCO exhibit (claims signoff is absent when it is present-but-mismatched), which is exactly the kind of written-record-vs-exhibit discrepancy this lens exists to catch, even in the auditors' own artifact. None of this proves the design is worthless -- the schema and process discipline are genuinely more rigorous than most such proposals -- but the claim that the controls 'fail closed' rests on a review gate that is documented, not deployed.
report.hypothesis_supported: H2 (fail-closed claimed but not enforced) is the best-supported rival: the decision-ledger's own documentation names human PR review as the backstop against a well-formed-but-verdict-carrying or narrative-satisfying entry, and that backstop is verified absent (no branch protection, no required reviewers, zero reviews on PR #35 itself). This also partially feeds H5 (validator is an inadequate oracle for anything beyond its fixtures) since C4's mechanical check has never touched a real entry.
report.hypothesis_killed: H3 (controls unreachable / never fire due to no new trigger) is not supported by this lens's evidence -- the schema, workflow wiring, and skill text clearly make the controls reachable in principle (they are bound into existing skill moments and CI runs the fixture batteries on every relevant PR); the failure found here is enforcement-teeth at the merge/audit boundary, not unreachability of the mechanism itself.
report.fix_or_move: Minimum fix set: (1) add branch protection or an org ruleset requiring ≥1 approving review + required status checks (including DCO) on main before treating 'human review' as an enforced C1/C5 backstop; (2) wire validate_examples.py (or an equivalent) into a real pre-write/pre-commit gate over actual '.ledger/entries.jsonl', not only committed fixtures, before crediting C4 as mechanically enforced; (3) correct the dossier's DCO evidence line to reflect signoff-email-mismatch rather than absent signoff; (4) either log the integration's own design decision as a real ledger-entry@1 or record the explicit skip per the skill's own floor. None of these are fatal to the design's merit, but until done, the 'fail-closed' and 'human-enforced' claims are policy text, not verified controls.

report.verdict: The PR is real (53 files, +2436/-27, verified in the worktree) but almost all of its added mass is unwired prose. Of the five controls, only C4 (recurrent-failure chain) has a genuinely discriminating mechanical check — and even that runs only against three static reference examples the same PR authored, never against a real .ledger/entries.jsonl. The parallel epistemic-process-trace@1 validator/behavioral-scorer/fixture harness (~700 lines) has zero production consumer: no SKILL.md emits or reads that object; its 8/8 and 12/12 PASS results only prove the checker can tell apart hand-written gold/bad JSON written in the same commit. The dossier's 'VERIFIED premises' PASS lines are true but narrower than they read — they verify tool self-consistency, not that any of C1/C2/C3/C5 fire during real work. Adjacent-possible reading: the dominant nearby variant is not a rewrite — it is (1) deleting or deferring the standalone trace scaffold, (2) pointing the one working oracle (decision-ledger's schema check) at real live ledger files instead of only static references, and (3) wiring the single best new idea (gauntlet's validation_kernel) into the schema/tests gauntlet already has. All three are 10-30 line edits that reuse machinery already proven in this repo, versus the ~2000 lines of new parallel scaffolding that currently enforces nothing against reality.
report.hypothesis_supported: H3 (unreachable/never fire) and H5 (inadequate/self-referential oracle) are both strongly supported by [V] evidence: zero SKILL.md references the new trace validator, and none of the pre-existing enforced gates (judge.py, lens.schema.json) were touched. H2 (fluent narrative can satisfy a control) is supported for C1/C2/C3/C5 (no truth-check exists at all) and partially for C4 (structural-only presence check accepts fabricated-but-well-formed chains).
report.hypothesis_killed: H4 (failure_chain conditional is under-specified/not mechanically validated) is refuted specifically for decision-ledger's schema: validate_examples.py implements the conditional correctly and includes planted negative fixtures that genuinely assert FAIL — the conditional logic itself is well-specified and mechanically checked, just only against static reference data (a distinct, narrower concern captured in F4, not H4's original shape).
report.fix_or_move: Ship this next, in order of cheapness-to-teeth ratio: (1) point decision-ledger's validate_examples.py glob at real repo-local .ledger/entries.jsonl files in CI, not just reference/example-*.json (F4); (2) add validation_kernel as a required field in gauntlet/roster/lens.schema.json plus one assertion in gauntlet/tests/run_tests.py (F3); (3) defer or delete the epistemic-process-trace@1 validator/behavioral-scorer/fixture scaffold until some skill actually emits that object in production (F2) — its current 8/8 and 12/12 PASS numbers should not be cited as evidence the five controls work, only that the checker script is internally consistent.

findings.claim: The research synthesis reaches a strong-language verdict ("Strongly supported") on its own self-authored evaluation rubric for a claim that is nearly unfalsifiable as stated, while the same document explicitly concedes the evidence needed to support the actually-consequential claim (that this changes agent behavior for the better) does not exist yet. This is confirmation bias: a favorable conclusion is reached and stated with confidence disproportionate to what was actually tested.
findings.reasoning: The synthesis doc defines its own 'derivation test' (S = trigger/representation/operation/evidence-source/stopping-rule/memory-update), applies it to itself, and grades the hypothesis 'the skills can be enhanced by ACT/DBT/CBT' as 'Strongly supported' on the basis that 'several concrete controls can be integrated and tested' [V docs/research/2026-07-22-psychology-epistemic-skills-synthesis.md:19]. But 'can be integrated and tested' is true of almost any additional structure and says nothing about whether the integration actually improves anything -- the document's own Limits section immediately undercuts the strength of that verdict: 'Psychotherapy mechanism research often identifies statistical or conceptual mediators without fully specified functional mechanisms' [V docs/research/2026-07-22-psychology-epistemic-skills-synthesis.md:246] and 'Evidence that a human therapeutic process works does not establish that the translated process improves language-agent behavior. The four-arm behavioral evaluation is required' [V docs/research/2026-07-22-psychology-epistemic-skills-synthesis.md:247]. The dossier itself confirms the causal claim remains open: 'Behavioral superiority of the integrated arm over baseline/2.8.0/psychology-only: NOT established (four-arm ablation not yet run at freeze time -- Phase 4)' [V docs/gauntlet-runs/epistemic-flexibility-v3-2026-07-22/dossier.md:62-63]. The strong verdict word ('Strongly supported') is doing rhetorical work that the underlying evidence does not license -- a classic sentence-level tell of motivated reasoning: the author wants continued confidence in a program already implemented, and grades their own rubric to deliver it.

findings.claim: The deterministic conformance battery and the behavioral scorer's gold/planted-bad traces were authored in the same session, by the same reasoning process, as the validator they are scored against -- with the design's own C3 principle (preregister before seeing the result, to limit post-hoc reinterpretation) never applied reflexively to the test suite itself. The '8/8' and '12/12' PASS counts are evidence the author's fixtures match the author's validator, not independent evidence the controls discriminate real good/bad traces.
findings.reasoning: C3 states: 'A result without a prior prediction is evidence, but it is weaker evidence against post-hoc reinterpretation' [V .gauntlet-subject/reference.md:52-54, identical to V plugins/epistemic-skills/skills/using-epistemic-skills/reference/epistemic-flexibility.md:52-54]. Yet the handoff admits no independent party constructed or ran these fixtures: 'Needs isolated concurrent exact-role agents behind a barrier and a separate arbitrator... This session has no generic isolated-subagent primitive and therefore did not simulate independence in one context' [V docs/handoffs/2026-07-22-epistemic-flexibility-v3.md:106-112]. The spec frames the battery itself as 'a protocol conformance smoke check, not a behavioral effectiveness measurement' [V .gauntlet-subject/spec.md:108] -- an honest label -- but the dossier's 'VERIFIED premises' table nonetheless lists 'protocol fixtures PASS 8/8' and 'behavioral scorer gold/bad PASS 12/12' as reproducible facts [V docs/gauntlet-runs/epistemic-flexibility-v3-2026-07-22/dossier.md:48-49] without the same caveat attached at the point of citation, inviting a reader to treat self-graded PASS as stronger evidence than it is. This is confirmation bias in test construction: an author who just finished writing a specification will, without any adversarial intent, generate 'bad' traces that fail for the reasons they already expect and 'good' traces that pass for the reasons they already expect -- systematically under-sampling the ambiguous, boundary-case traces that would actually stress-test the rule.

report.verdict: The strongest metatextual finding is not in the mechanics of C1-C5 but in the authors' own epistemic conduct while building a tool whose entire purpose is enforcing better epistemic conduct: (1) a research-question verdict is graded 'Strongly supported' on a self-authored rubric while the document's own later sections and the dossier concede the behaviorally consequential claim is unestablished (F1); (2) the conformance and behavioral self-tests are constructed, implemented, and scored by the same undivided reasoning process the design's own C3 principle warns against trusting without independent preregistration (F2); (3) nine clinical-psychology citations lend borrowed scientific authority to a design that, by the shipped code's own structure, never actually depends on that literature (F3). findings.claim: The C4 failure_chain conditional is grafted onto a schema (ledger-entry@1) whose founding design (PR #32, spec #31) established a specific paired-fence pattern — every schema-unenforceable semantic requirement gets an explicit human-review checklist item in reference/README.md (the 'never-instruct boundary... human-enforced, not schema-checkable' item). PR #35 adds exactly one new checklist item ('Failure chain remains descriptive,' guarding against the chain being read as an authorization) but adds none for the actually load-bearing, self-attested antecedent: whether `recurrence_risk: true` was set honestly for a correction that in fact carries recurrence risk. The schema's `allOf` conditional only fires once that boolean is present and true — an agent can dodge the entire C4 apparatus by simply never writing `recurrence_risk: true`, and nothing in the repo's own established review discipline (the pattern this PR otherwise imitates) is aimed at catching that omission.
findings.reasoning: PR #32's founding decision-ledger design paired every schema-unenforceable semantic claim with a named human checklist item (the never-instruct boundary). PR #35 extends the same schema and reuses the same README checklist section, but only replicates half the pattern: it guards the chain's content (non-authorizing) but not the trigger condition (honest recurrence_risk marking) that gates whether the chain is required at all. This is the Chesterton-fence question in reverse — the fence ('unenforceable semantics get a checklist line') was understood well enough to be partially reproduced, but the single most consequential half of it was dropped without acknowledgment.

Racy pair: writer A reads current head X, appends E1{supersedes:[X]}; writer B, interleaved before A's append is visible, also reads head X and appends E2{supersedes:[X]}. Both entries individually validate against every shipped check; the store now has two 'heads' pointing at the same superseded id, and nothing in this PR's contract says which one continuity-verify or a gauntlet dossier must treat as current. This is exactly the surface C4 makes more consequential (recurrent-failure corrections are the multi-session, multi-agent scenario where this race is most likely), yet C4 ships with zero new teeth for it.

findings.claim: The new `.github/workflows/epistemic-flexibility.yml` triggers on broad path globs for both `pull_request` and `push: main` with no `concurrency:` group/cancel-in-progress key, so successive pushes to the same branch spawn independent, uncancelled overlapping runs — but this is resource contention, not (on reachability check) a merge-safety hole, since GitHub required-status-checks bind to the specific head SHA rather than 'latest run of this workflow name.'
findings.reasoning: The full 64-line workflow has no `concurrency:` block [V .github/workflows/epistemic-flexibility.yml:1-64]. Per my persona's own bias warning, I checked the reachability of the 'stale-PASS-overwrites-newer-FAIL' race and could not construct it under standard GitHub Actions semantics: required checks are evaluated against the exact merge/head SHA, and a run triggered by an older push is keyed to the older SHA, not the newer one. So the credible failure mode narrows to wasted CI-minutes/runner-queue contention on a `docs/**`-triggered, frequently-firing workflow, not an incorrect merge gate.

report.verdict: From the concurrency-interleaving lens: this PR is overwhelmingly prose/schema/fixture content with no new concurrent-state-mutation code, so most classic race/TOCTOU/idempotency vectors are structurally absent — a fair result, verified rather than assumed (CI-4). But the one place this PR does touch genuinely shared, multi-writer state — the decision-ledger's supersedes/head/id invariants that C4 (recurrent-failure chain) newly depends on more heavily — remains exactly as unenforced and untested after this PR as before it. The PR ships six new validators and dozens of fixtures for single-document shape-conformance, and zero for the one cross-document, multi-writer invariant its own SKILL.md and schema explicitly name as required ('single-writer-or-locked', 'acyclic graph with a unique head', 'store-level invariant, not schema-checkable'). That gap is real, concretely constructible (CI-1), and cheaply falsifiable — it should gate C4's claim to be production-ready for the multi-session/cross-device use this collection is designed for, even though it does not gate the PR's narrower claim of internal shape-conformance.
